name: CER01A Ceremony
blurb:
LA's third Opening Ceremony — after 1932 and 1984 — unfolds beneath the translucent canopy of SoFi Stadium, reborn as "2028 Stadium" for the occasion. Two hundred nations parade into the California evening while the Hollywood Hills frame the backdrop.

The city that invented the modern entertainment industry gets to stage the world's grandest spectacle. The cauldron lighting alone will be talked about for decades — and given that this is LA, the list of people who could light it is longer and more star-studded than anywhere else on earth.

name: CER02 Ceremony
blurb:
The Coliseum becomes the only venue in Olympic history to host three Closing Ceremonies. The 1984 cauldron still stands as a monument to LA's Olympic legacy, and this is where Brisbane 2032 receives the flame.

The peristyle end that watched Jesse Owens, Carl Lewis, and Muhammad Ali will witness the final act of LA28. Closing ceremonies carry an emotion that opening nights can't replicate — the weight of two weeks, the friendships forged, the last time this city will glow under the rings for a generation.

The second subdivision typically features the defending Olympic champion, and Daiki Hashimoto will deliver. Japan's gymnastics machine rarely falters in qualification, and the afternoon crowd witnesses the precision that's made Hashimoto the sport's most consistent performer for three years.

Stephen Nedoroscik could appear here — pommel horse specialists always bring theater to qualification.

If you thought the Paris crowds were loud, picture 18,000 people in DTLA Arena when Biles mounts the beam. This marathon session is where Olympic storylines are born and where the all-around, team, and apparatus finals all begin to take shape.

name: GAR07 Artistic Gymnastics
blurb:
The final women's subdivision closes out qualification under the lights. Jade Carey anchors Team USA's depth — the Tokyo vault champion who brings veteran composure when the pressure peaks.

Late-night gymnastics carries its own electricity. Olympic berths and apparatus final spots are on the line, and every routine could change someone's career forever.

contenders.name: Jade Carey
contenders.note: Tokyo vault champion bringing clutch experience to LA

contenders.name: Ellie Black
contenders.note: Canadian veteran making her likely final Olympic push

contenders.name: Georgia Godwin
contenders.note: All-around threat who thrives in high-pressure moments

name: GAR08 Artistic Gymnastics
blurb:
Japan stunned everyone by winning team gold in Paris. Competing in LA changes the equation for Team USA — the American men haven't won Olympic team gold since 2008, and a home crowd could be the difference.

Daiki Hashimoto leads Japan's title defense while China lurks as the team that could spoil everyone's plans. Three hours of team gymnastics where every dismount matters and no lead is safe.

contenders.name: Team USA
contenders.note: Home team seeking first Olympic team gold since Beijing 2008

contenders.name: Team Japan
contenders.note: Defending champions led by two-time Olympic AA winner Hashimoto

contenders.name: Team China
contenders.note: Gymnastics powerhouse with world-class depth across all events

name: GAR09 Artistic Gymnastics
blurb:
The US women's team final is THE Olympic gymnastics event. They haven't lost this competition since Beijing 2008, and Simone Biles competing at home makes them nearly unbeatable.

But Rebeca Andrade leads Brazil's charge, and Italy arrives fresh off their Paris breakthrough. When 18,000 people are on their feet for every American routine, this transcends gymnastics — it becomes destiny unfolding in real time.

contenders.name: Team USA
contenders.note: Undefeated in team finals since 2008, led by GOAT Simone Biles at home

contenders.name: Team Brazil
contenders.note: Rebeca Andrade's squad could pull off the upset of the Olympics

contenders.name: Team Italy
contenders.note: European champions with momentum from their Paris breakthrough

name: GAR10 Artistic Gymnastics
blurb:
Six events, two and a half hours, one Olympic champion. Daiki Hashimoto seeks history as the first man to win three straight Olympic all-around titles since the 1960s.

Zhang Boheng didn't become world champion by accident, and the men's all-around is pure athleticism — no music, no flash, just the ultimate test of who is the most complete male gymnast alive.

contenders.name: Daiki Hashimoto
contenders.note: Going for unprecedented third consecutive Olympic all-around title

contenders.name: Zhang Boheng
contenders.note: World champion with the difficulty to dethrone Hashimoto

contenders.name: Illia Kovtun
contenders.note: European champion bringing massive scores and underdog hunger

name: GAR11 Artistic Gymnastics
blurb:
The most-watched event of the Olympic Games. Simone Biles writing the final chapter of her legendary career at home — four apparatuses, two and a half hours — to determine the best female gymnast on earth.

Rebeca Andrade is the only person who consistently pushes Biles when medals are on the line. Suni Lee lurks as the defending champion who knows how to win when everything's at stake. This is the event that stops the world.

contenders.name: Simone Biles
contenders.note: GOAT seeking all-around gold at her home Olympics

contenders.name: Rebeca Andrade
contenders.note: Only gymnast who consistently challenges Biles when it counts

contenders.name: Suni Lee
contenders.note: Defending Olympic all-around champion with championship experience

name: GAR12 Artistic Gymnastics
blurb:
Apparatus finals begin with vault and pommel horse. Stephen Nedoroscik — America's most beloved gymnast after his viral Paris moment — brings his Clark Kent pommel horse routine to LA, where he could become the first American to win this event since 1984.

Women's vault features pure power: Simone Biles will likely unleash her signature Yurchenko double pike that no one else dares attempt.

contenders.name: Simone Biles
contenders.note: Vault goddess with moves literally named after her

contenders.name: Rebeca Andrade
contenders.note: Paris vault champion who can match Biles' power

contenders.name: Stephen Nedoroscik
contenders.note: Pommel horse king seeking America's first gold on event since 1984

name: GAR13 Artistic Gymnastics
blurb:
Women's beam finals are the most nerve-wracking two hours in Olympic sport — four inches wide, four feet high, and careers decided by millimeters. Alice D'Amato proved in Paris that ice runs through her veins.

Simone Biles has been refining her beam routine for this exact moment. Men's parallel bars rounds out the session with upper-body artistry that showcases gymnastics at its most elegant.

contenders.name: Alice D'Amato
contenders.note: Paris beam champion with nerves of absolute steel

contenders.name: Simone Biles
contenders.note: Beam demon who's conquered her biggest Olympic nemesis

contenders.name: Zhou Yaqin
contenders.note: Chinese beam specialist with textbook precision

name: GAR14 Artistic Gymnastics
blurb:
The final individual apparatus finals feature gymnastics' two most spectacular disciplines. Women's floor combines tumbling, artistry, and pure entertainment — this is where Rebeca Andrade beat Biles in Paris and where Jordan Chiles sparked controversy.

Men's high bar closes the gymnastics program with death-defying release moves nine feet off the ground. The perfect finale to two weeks of gymnastics at LA28.

contenders.name: Rebeca Andrade
contenders.note: Floor queen who outdueled Biles in Paris with signature Brazilian flair

contenders.name: Jordan Chiles
contenders.note: Controversial Paris bronze medalist seeking redemption on home soil

contenders.name: Tin Srbic
contenders.note: High bar specialist with release moves that defy gravity

name: GAR15 Artistic Gymnastics
desc: Mixed Team Final (Format and Duration Pending FIG Approval)
blurb:
The mixed team event makes its Olympic debut in LA. Picture the world's best male and female gymnasts competing together for national glory in a format that could revolutionize the sport.

Nobody knows exactly what to expect, which makes it absolutely unmissable. Olympic history written in real time — and you'll be able to say you were there when gymnastics changed forever.

name: SWA01 Artistic Swimming
blurb: The technical routine is all precision and difficulty, where a single timing error between partners can shatter Olympic dreams. This is your chance to see artistic swimming up close before the crowds and prices explode for the finals.

contenders.name: Chen Yuxi / Shi Tingmao
contenders.note: Paris champions bringing their flawless timing to LA's pool.

contenders.name: Kate Shortman / Isabelle Thorpe
contenders.note: Britain's breakthrough duo looking to ride momentum from European success.

contenders.name: Jaime Czarkowski / Jacklyn Luu
contenders.note: Home crowd darlings with technical precision that could surprise the field.

name: SWA02 Artistic Swimming
blurb: The duet free routine is where artistic swimming transforms into pure art — four minutes of underwater ballet where partners must breathe as one. Unlike the rigid technical format, here creativity rules, and teams can showcase signature moves that define their identity. Watch China's defending champions navigate the pressure while Team USA feeds off Long Beach's energy in what could be the sport's most emotionally charged preliminary round.

contenders.name: China Duet
contenders.note: The gold standard in free routine innovation and execution.

contenders.name: Russia Duet
contenders.note: Masters of dramatic storytelling through synchronized movement.

contenders.name: USA Duet
contenders.note: California-trained athletes with home pool advantage.

name: SWA03 Artistic Swimming
blurb:
Eight swimmers, one heartbeat — team technical is artistic swimming's ultimate test of collective precision. Every lift, every spin, every breath must happen in perfect unison, or the routine crumbles before 15,000 spectators.

China's powerhouse team enters as the squad to beat, and Team USA's deep talent pool will feed off the energy of performing in their backyard at Long Beach Aquatics Center.

contenders.name: China Team
contenders.note: Paris gold medalists with unmatched technical difficulty scores.

contenders.name: USA Team
contenders.note: Home crowd favorites trained in these exact California waters.

contenders.name: Japan Team
contenders.note: Technical perfectionists with intricate choreography.

contenders.name: Spain Team
contenders.note: European champions known for their flawless synchronization.

name: SWA04 Artistic Swimming
blurb: Team free routine preliminary — this is where artistic swimming becomes pure theater, eight athletes creating an underwater symphony that defies physics. The Chinese team will unveil their championship routine for the first time on American soil, while Team USA gets to perform their California-inspired choreography in front of family and friends. Witness aquatic artistry that took four years to perfect.

contenders.name: China Team
contenders.note: Defending champions with the sport's most innovative free routines.

contenders.name: USA Team
contenders.note: LA-based training gives them intimate knowledge of this venue.

contenders.name: Italy Team
contenders.note: Artistic powerhouse blending Mediterranean flair with technical mastery.

contenders.name: Australia Team
contenders.note: Southern Hemisphere's best with surprising acrobatic elements.

name: SWA05 Artistic Swimming
blurb: The acrobatic routine is artistic swimming's newest and wildest event — think human pyramids and impossible throws, all performed while treading water. Teams launch swimmers skyward in formations that seem to defy both gravity and common sense, creating spectacular splashes and gasps from the crowd. This preliminary round offers your best chance to witness artistic swimming's most death-defying discipline without paying finals prices.

contenders.name: China Team
contenders.note: Acrobatic innovators pushing the boundaries of what's possible in water.

contenders.name: USA Team
contenders.note: American power and athleticism translates perfectly to explosive acrobatics.

contenders.name: Ukraine Team
contenders.note: Gymnastics background creates stunning aerial sequences.

contenders.name: Canada Team
contenders.note: North American rivals with surprisingly dynamic acrobatic skills.

name: ATH01 Athletics (Track & Field)
desc: W/M 100m Prelim & R1; W Shot Put Qual; W 5000m R1; M Pole Vault Qual; W 800m R1; W Hammer Throw Qual
blurb:
The Coliseum comes alive with opening-day track and field. Six events unfold simultaneously as the world's fastest humans begin their campaigns — Noah Lyles and Sha'Carri Richardson run their first 100m heats on the same track where Carl Lewis launched his four-gold dynasty in 1984.

Ryan Crouser begins his quest for a historic third consecutive shot put gold. The 100m heats are deceptively crucial — one false start or stumble and the world's fastest humans watch the final from the stands.

contenders.name: Noah Lyles
contenders.note: Home favorite chasing the 100/200 double on familiar turf.

contenders.name: Sha'Carri Richardson
contenders.note: The crowd will erupt every time she steps on the track.

contenders.name: Kishane Thompson
contenders.note: Jamaican heir who nearly beat Lyles in Paris.

contenders.name: Ryan Crouser
contenders.note: Shot put legend starting his march toward Olympic history.

name: ATH02 Athletics (Track & Field)
desc: M 400mH R1; M High Jump & Discus Qual; W Triple Jump Qual; W 100m Semi & Final; W 400mH R1; W Shot Put Final; M 10,000m Final
blurb:
The first Saturday night at the Coliseum, and it delivers immediately: Sha'Carri Richardson racing for 100m gold in front of 78,000 people, on home soil, under the lights. The women's 100m final alone justifies the evening.

Sifan Hassan returns for the 10,000m final, and a loaded session showcases why the Coliseum remains the cathedral of track and field. Three gold medals decided as the California sun sets behind the peristyle.

contenders.name: Sha'Carri Richardson
contenders.note: Paris silver medalist with home crowd advantage.

contenders.name: Julien Alfred
contenders.note: Defending 100m champion from tiny Saint Lucia.

contenders.name: Sifan Hassan
contenders.note: Multi-event wizard attempting another distance masterpiece.

contenders.name: Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone
contenders.note: Could make rare 400m hurdles preliminary appearance.

name: ATH03 Athletics (Track & Field)
desc: M Decathlon (100m, LJ, Shot Put); W Discus Qual; M Shot Put Qual; W 800m Rep; Mixed 4x400m R1; M Long Jump Qual
blurb:
Decathlon day one begins — ten events over two days to crown the World's Greatest Athlete. The morning captures the opening salvo: 100m, long jump, and shot put, where athletes must master three completely different disciplines before lunch.

Kevin Mayer could be making his final Olympic run while the mixed 4×400m relay first round adds relay drama to the morning program. Watching multi-eventers transition between sprinting, jumping, and throwing is pure athletic theater — and the decathlon is the event that rewards the most complete human being.

contenders.name: Kevin Mayer
contenders.note: Former world record holder making potentially his last Olympic run.

contenders.name: Ryan Crouser
contenders.note: Shot put qualifying ahead of his gold medal defense.

contenders.name: Noah Lyles
contenders.note: Could appear in mixed 4x400m relay rounds.

name: ATH04 Athletics (Track & Field)
desc: M Decathlon (High Jump, 400m); M Shot Put Final; M 1500m R1; M 100m Semi & Final; W Hammer Final; Mixed 4x400m Final
blurb:
The most iconic session at any Olympic Games. The men's 100m final — roughly nine seconds that crown the fastest human alive — has defined the Olympics since Jesse Owens in 1936. It happens here, inside the same Coliseum where Carl Lewis won the event in 1984.

The session builds methodically: the 100m semifinals narrow the field to eight finalists, Ryan Crouser chases a historic third straight shot put gold, and the mixed 4×400m relay final — just its second Olympic appearance — adds another layer. Then, as the California evening settles over the peristyle, eight sprinters crouch into the blocks.

Same venue. Same event. Same electricity — 44 years later.

contenders.name: Noah Lyles
contenders.note: Defending champion chasing history on home soil.

contenders.name: Kishane Thompson
contenders.note: Jamaican speedster who ran Lyles to the wire in Paris.

contenders.name: Letsile Tebogo
contenders.note: 19-year-old Botswanan phenom with closing speed.

contenders.name: Ryan Crouser
contenders.note: Shot put titan going for unprecedented third gold.

name: ATH05 Athletics (Track & Field)
desc: M Decathlon (110mH, Discus, Pole Vault); W 3000m SC R1; M 400m R1; W Triple Jump Final; M 400mH Rep; W 400mH Rep
blurb:
Decathlon day two unfolds with five events determining the World's Greatest Athlete, while the women's triple jump final showcases explosive power in the field.

The 110m hurdles and pole vault are always the decathlon's most dramatic chapters — one bad clearance can torpedo months of preparation. Morning sessions strip away the evening-session spectacle and give you the decathlon's tactical chess match at its most intimate.

contenders.name: Kevin Mayer
contenders.note: Veteran Frenchman chasing his first Olympic decathlon gold.

contenders.name: Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone
contenders.note: 400m hurdles world record holder in repechage rounds.

contenders.name: Femke Bol
contenders.note: Dutch hurdles and relay star advancing through rounds.

name: ATH06 Athletics (Track & Field)
desc: M Pole Vault Final; M Decathlon (Javelin, 1500m Final); Mixed 4x100m R1 & Final; M 1500m Rep; W 800m Semi
blurb:
Mondo Duplantis owns Monday night. The Swedish pole vault prodigy who keeps rewriting his own world record will have the Coliseum mesmerized as he clears heights no one else can reach.

The decathlon reaches its emotional climax with the 1500m — exhausted multi-eventers running the final event of a two-day war, knowing every second reshuffles the medal standings. The brand-new mixed 4×100m relay makes its Olympic debut. This session is anchored by the most compelling individual athlete in all of track and field.

contenders.name: Mondo Duplantis
contenders.note: World record holder who turns pole vault into must-see TV.

contenders.name: Kevin Mayer
contenders.note: Completing his decathlon quest with the punishing 1500m.

contenders.name: Noah Lyles
contenders.note: Could anchor USA in the debut mixed 4x100m relay.

contenders.name: Sha'Carri Richardson
contenders.note: Potential mixed relay leg after her 100m campaign.

name: ATH07 Athletics (Track & Field)
desc: Women's Pole Vault Qual; Men's 3000m SC R1; Women's 400m R1; Men's Triple Jump Qual; Men's 110mH R1
blurb:
Tuesday morning belongs to the hurdlers and pole vaulters finding their rhythm in qualifying rounds. The 110m hurdles prelims showcase the event's signature blend of speed and technical precision — these rounds often produce the deepest fields and closest margins.

Qualifying sessions offer the most intimate way to study world-class technique before the chaos of finals. The athletes are focused, the Coliseum is more relaxed, and you're watching some of the most gifted humans on earth fine-tune their craft.

contenders.name: Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone
contenders.note: Could double from 400m hurdles into the 400m flat.

contenders.name: Mondo Duplantis
contenders.note: Swedish pole vault superstar in qualifying rounds.

contenders.name: Grant Holloway
contenders.note: 110m hurdles world champion and record holder.

name: ATH08 Athletics (Track & Field)
desc: Men's 200m R1; Men's High Jump Final; Men's Discus Final; Women's 5000m Final; M/W 400mH Semi; Men's 1500m Semi; Women's 800m Final
blurb:
Five finals in a single evening session — women's 5000m, women's 800m, men's high jump, men's discus, plus the 400m hurdles semifinals that will decide who reaches the finals. This is the deepest, most stacked session on the entire Coliseum schedule.

Faith Kipyegon advances through her 1500m semifinal, Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone runs the 400m hurdles semi, and Noah Lyles opens his 200m campaign. More gold-medal-worthy performances packed into three hours than most entire sports produce across the full Games.

contenders.name: Faith Kipyegon
contenders.note: Two-time 1500m champion seeking her third straight gold.

contenders.name: Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone
contenders.note: 400m hurdles world record holder in semifinal action.

contenders.name: Jakob Ingebrigtsen
contenders.note: Norwegian 1500m tactical genius and defending champion.

contenders.name: Sifan Hassan
contenders.note: Could be doubling between distance events again.

name: ATH09 Athletics (Track & Field)
desc: Men's Hammer Qual; W/M 400m Rep; Women's Long Jump Qual; Men's 200m Rep; Women's 3000m SC Final
blurb:
Wednesday morning features the women's 3000m steeplechase final — seven and a half laps of barriers and water jumps that produce the most spectacular crashes and comebacks in track and field.

The steeplechase rewards both distance-running stamina and hurdling technique, making it one of the most unpredictable finals on the Olympic program. Repechage rounds for the 200m and 400m add sprint drama to the morning.

contenders.name: Conseslus Kipruto
contenders.note: Former Olympic steeplechase champion looking to reclaim his title.

contenders.name: Ryan Crouser
contenders.note: Hammer throw qualifying before his shot put title defense.

contenders.name: Noah Lyles
contenders.note: Potential 200m repechage if needed after opening rounds.

name: ATH10 Athletics (Track & Field)
desc: Men's 110mH Rep; Women's Discus Final; Women's 1500m R1; Men's Long Jump Final; Women's 200m R1; Men's 1500m Final; Men's 400mH Final
blurb:
Jakob Ingebrigtsen versus Josh Kerr in the 1500m final — the most anticipated middle-distance showdown since Coe and Ovett in 1980. Faith Kipyegon could complete a historic three-peat on the same evening.

Miltiadis Tentoglou defends his long jump title between the metric mile drama and Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone's 400m hurdles final, which crowns the queen of the most technically demanding sprint on the program. Three of the Olympics' most technically precise athletes in one session.

contenders.name: Jakob Ingebrigtsen
contenders.note: Norwegian 1500m king with the most devastating kick in the sport.

contenders.name: Josh Kerr
contenders.note: British miler who upset Jakob in Paris and has the speed to do it again.

contenders.name: Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone
contenders.note: World record holder defending her 400m hurdles crown.

contenders.name: Miltiadis Tentoglou
contenders.note: Back-to-back Olympic long jump champion with ice in his veins.

name: ATH11 Athletics (Track & Field)
desc: Women's Javelin Qual; Women's High Jump Qual; Men's 800m R1; Women's 200m Rep; Men's 3000m SC Final
blurb:
The men's steeplechase final delivers the Olympics' most chaotic distance race — 28 barriers and seven water jumps that separate the brave from the broken.

Kenyan dominance meets Norwegian tactical brilliance while qualifying rounds set up weekend fireworks in the javelin and high jump. The steeplechase has produced some of the Games' most dramatic finishes — and morning finals carry their own special tension.

contenders.name: Lamecha Girma
contenders.note: Ethiopian world record holder who dominates when healthy.

contenders.name: Conseslus Kipruto
contenders.note: Former Olympic champion representing Kenyan steeplechase tradition.

contenders.name: Kelsey-Lee Barber
contenders.note: Australian javelin world champion in qualifying rounds.

name: ATH12 Athletics (Track & Field)
desc: Women's Pole Vault Final; Women's 1500m Rep; Men's Hammer Final; W/M 400m Semi; Men's 110mH Semi; Men's 200m Semi; Women's 400mH Final
blurb:
Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone caps Thursday evening by defending her 400m hurdles world record and Olympic title — the most technically demanding sprint on the program, and she makes it look effortless.

The women's pole vault final runs alongside the hammer throw, while semifinal rounds determine who reaches the weekend's sprint showdowns. McLaughlin-Levrone versus Femke Bol is the rivalry that defines modern women's athletics.

contenders.name: Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone
contenders.note: Defending 400m hurdles champion and world record holder.

contenders.name: Femke Bol
contenders.note: Dutch hurdles star who pushes McLaughlin-Levrone to historic times.

contenders.name: Mondo Duplantis
contenders.note: Pole vault superstar potentially competing in women's final as exhibition.

contenders.name: Noah Lyles
contenders.note: 200m semifinal action in his specialty event.

name: ATH13 Athletics (Track & Field)
desc: Women's Heptathlon (100mH, High Jump); Men's 5000m R1; Women's 100mH R1; Men's Triple Jump Final; Men's 800m Rep
blurb:
Heptathlon day one begins the quest to crown the world's greatest female athlete over seven events and two days. The triple jump final showcases the most explosive single effort in field events — three phases of flight that can span over 15 meters.

The heptathlon's 100m hurdles and high jump opening events reveal the incredible versatility required to compete at this level. These are the athletes who refuse to specialize, and their breadth of talent is staggering.

contenders.name: Nafissatou Thiam
contenders.note: Belgian heptathlon legend chasing her third consecutive Olympic title.

contenders.name: Anna Hall
contenders.note: American heptathlete with home crowd advantage.

contenders.name: Katarina Johnson-Thompson
contenders.note: British multi-event star and former world champion.

name: ATH14 Athletics (Track & Field)
desc: Women's Heptathlon (Shot Put, 200m); Men's Javelin Qual; Women's 1500m Semi; Women's 200m Semi; Women's 10,000m Final; Men's 110mH Final; Men's 200m Final
blurb:
Friday night delivers Noah Lyles in the 200m final — his signature event, the one that rewards the artistry of bend running, the one where he's been virtually unbeatable when healthy. On home soil, under the Coliseum lights, this could be a performance for the ages.

Sifan Hassan returns for the women's 10,000m final. The 110m hurdles final crowns the fastest hurdler alive. This is the last Friday night of track and field at LA28.

contenders.name: Noah Lyles
contenders.note: 200m world record threat and three-time world champion in his best event.

contenders.name: Letsile Tebogo
contenders.note: Defending 200m champion who owns the fastest time in the world.

contenders.name: Sifan Hassan
contenders.note: Distance running legend potentially doubling in the 10K.

contenders.name: Grant Holloway
contenders.note: 110m hurdles world champion hunting his first Olympic gold.

name: ATH15 Athletics (Track & Field)
desc: W Heptathlon (LJ, Javelin, 800m Final); W 100mH Rep; M 800m Semi; W Long Jump Final; M 4x100m R1; W 400m Final; M 400m Final; W 200m Final
blurb:
Championship Saturday at the Coliseum. Seven finals, including the relay events that could rewrite the record books.

The heptathlon concludes with its brutal 800m — exhausted athletes running for their lives knowing every second reshuffles the medal standings. Gabby Thomas and the American women race for 200m gold on home soil. This session has produced more iconic Olympic moments than any other in track and field history.

contenders.name: Gabby Thomas
contenders.note: Defending 200m champion and the face of American sprinting.

contenders.name: Julien Alfred
contenders.note: Saint Lucian speedster potentially doubling from 100m to 200m.

contenders.name: USA Men's 4x100m
contenders.note: Lyles anchoring could produce fireworks in front of home crowd.

contenders.name: USA Women's 4x100m
contenders.note: Richardson and Thomas leading a loaded American relay squad.

name: ATH16 Athletics (Track & Field)
desc: W 4x400m R1; W High Jump Final; M 4x400m R1; W Javelin Final; W 4x100m R1; W 100mH Semi; M 800m Final; W 1500m Final; M 4x100m Final
blurb:
The women's 1500m final and men's 800m final headline the most stacked evening session at the Coliseum. Faith Kipyegon hunts her historic three-peat while tactical chaos explodes over two laps in the men's 800m.

The men's 4×100m relay final caps the night with Team USA chasing redemption after years of baton-pass disasters, while the women's high jump final delivers its signature drama as the bar climbs toward record territory. Sunday night at the Coliseum is when legends are made.

contenders.name: Faith Kipyegon
contenders.note: Going for an unprecedented third straight Olympic 1500m title.

contenders.name: Emmanuel Wanyonyi
contenders.note: 800m world champion with the finishing kick to burn anyone.

contenders.name: Marco Arop
contenders.note: Paris silver medalist who thrives in tactical 800m battles.

contenders.name: Yaroslava Mahuchikh
contenders.note: High jump world record holder seeking her first Olympic gold.

contenders.name: Noah Lyles
contenders.note: Anchor leg for Team USA's 4×100m relay redemption story.

name: ATH17 Athletics (Track & Field)
desc: M Javelin Final; W 4x100m Final; M 5000m Final; W 100mH Final; M 4x400m Final; W 4x400m Final
blurb:
The final night of track at the Coliseum. The women's 4×100m relay with Sha'Carri Richardson anchoring Team USA on home soil. Jakob Ingebrigtsen's tactical mastery in the 5000m. Both 4×400m relay finals closing the show.

When the last runner crosses the finish line, the Coliseum's track and field chapter at LA28 is complete. This is the session that will define American track and field for a generation — and the last time the Coliseum hosts Olympic athletics until who knows when.

contenders.name: Sha'Carri Richardson
contenders.note: The crowd favorite anchoring Team USA's 4×100m relay on home soil.

contenders.name: Jakob Ingebrigtsen
contenders.note: 5000m tactical genius who saves his best for Olympic finals.

contenders.name: Masai Russell
contenders.note: Paris 100m hurdles champion defending her crown at home.

contenders.name: Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone
contenders.note: Could anchor the 4×400m after her 400m individual brilliance.

contenders.name: Femke Bol
contenders.note: Dutch relay anchor with the speed to challenge anyone on the final leg.

name: ATH18 Athletics (Race Walk)
blurb:
Race walking is the Olympics' most technically demanding endurance event — one foot must always touch the ground, both knees must stay straight, and three red cards means disqualification. The half-marathon distance tests tactical intelligence as much as fitness.

Spain's Alvaro Martin and Ecuador's Brian Daniel Pintado have been trading world titles, while Yang Jiayu makes the discipline look effortless. The race finishes inside the Coliseum — the same dramatic entrance that marathon runners have used since 1984.

contenders.name: Alvaro Martin
contenders.note: Paris 20km champion from Spain's race walk dynasty

contenders.name: Brian Daniel Pintado
contenders.note: Ecuador's first Olympic walking champion, hungry for repeat gold

contenders.name: Yang Jiayu
contenders.note: World record holder who makes 20km look like a warm-up

contenders.name: Maria Perez
contenders.note: Spanish world champion and master of race walk tactics

name: ATH19 Athletics (Marathon)
blurb:
Venice Beach at sunrise. The world's fastest women tackle 26.2 miles along the California coast — and it's free to watch from the roadside.

Tigst Assefa's world record of 2:11:53 has redefined what's possible in the women's marathon. Sifan Hassan proved in Paris that distance running's boldest athletes can do things nobody imagined. The early start means cool ocean air, golden morning light, and a finish inside the Coliseum where Olympic legends are made.

contenders.name: Tigst Assefa
contenders.note: World record holder who runs marathons like they're 10Ks

contenders.name: Sifan Hassan
contenders.note: The Paris triple-threat looking to defend her marathon gold

contenders.name: Peres Jepchirchir
contenders.note: Olympic champion who thrives in championship races

contenders.name: Ruth Chepngetich
contenders.note: The first woman under 2:10, when the course is certified

contenders.name: Des Linden
contenders.note: Boston Marathon legend racing her final Olympics on home soil

name: ATH20 Athletics (Marathon)
blurb:
Eliud Kipchoge's final Olympic marathon. The greatest distance runner in history, at 40 years old, running through the streets of Los Angeles one last time.

Tamirat Tola shocked Paris with a scorching 2:06:26 victory. Kelvin Kiptum's 2:00:35 world record — set before his tragic death — still haunts every elite marathon field. Start at Venice Beach in the early morning, then follow the race as it builds toward the spine-tingling finish inside the Coliseum, where every marathon runner enters as a gladiator.

name: BSB01 Baseball
blurb:
Baseball returns to the Olympics for the first time since 2008, and Dodger Stadium is the stage. If MLB releases its players, these opening pool games could feature the most talented rosters the sport has ever assembled — wearing national colors instead of franchise logos.

With only six teams in a round-robin format, every pitch matters from game one. The daytime slot at Chavez Ravine has its own magic — warm California sun, the San Gabriel Mountains beyond center field, and history being written at one of America's most storied ballparks.

Pool play sounds routine until you remember that every result shapes the knockout bracket. With potentially All-Star-caliber rosters representing their countries, even a group-stage game at Dodger Stadium becomes an event.

name: BSB12 Baseball
blurb:
Baseball hasn't been at the Olympics since 2008, and MLB players have never competed. That could change at Dodger Stadium in 2028.

Commissioner Manfred says they're close to a deal. Bryce Harper, Paul Skenes, Tarik Skubal — they all want in. If it happens, this will be the first time the world's best baseball players compete for Olympic gold, and they'll do it at Dodger Stadium — the sport's most iconic active venue.

Olympic baseball was a demonstration sport here in 1984, drawing a sellout of 55,235 for the gold medal game. This is a homecoming 44 years in the making. If Ohtani plays for Japan at his home park for a gold medal? That's the kind of moment that defines a generation of the sport.

Every combination echoes through the room. The crowd sits close enough to feel the intensity of two people fighting for the ultimate prize in sport.

name: BOX23 Boxing - Finals
blurb:
Four gold medals in one session — Olympic champions crowned every 30 minutes for two hours straight. From featherweight precision to middleweight power, DTLA Arena becomes the center of the boxing universe.

This is the first Olympics with full gender parity in boxing — seven weight classes each for men and women — so two of tonight's four finals are women's bouts. While basketball fills 18,000-seat venues and soccer packs 90,000 into the Rose Bowl, boxing's championship night puts you close enough to hear the leather land.

The intimacy is the point. The compressed energy of a tiny room, the national pride, the drama of two people fighting for the ultimate prize in sport — and when it's over, you walk out into the DTLA night.

name: BOX24 Boxing - Finals
blurb:
Boxing's grand finale: four gold medals in the sport's most prestigious weight classes. Women's featherweight and middleweight, men's welterweight and light welterweight — the divisions where power meets artistry.

DTLA Arena's 3,600 seats witness the final punches of Olympic boxing at LA28. Champions crowned, anthems played, and the most intimate gold medal experience at these entire Games comes to a close.

name: CSL01 Canoe Slalom
desc: M/W Kayak Single Heats - 1st and 2nd Run
blurb: a chance to witness precision paddling at its finest — this is where Jessica Fox and the world's best kayakers attack the Oklahoma City course for the first time. Two runs each, best time advances, and you'll see raw speed through those hanging gates without the pressure-cooker atmosphere of finals. Perfect for canoe slalom newcomers who want to understand why missing a gate by inches can ruin an Olympic dream.

contenders.name: Jessica Fox
contenders.note: The GOAT looking to add another gold to her Paris double

contenders.note: Tokyo champion and Fox's biggest threat

name: CSL02 Canoe Slalom
desc: Women's Kayak Single Semifinal and Final
blurb:
Jessica Fox vs. the field for Olympic gold — this IS the women's kayak showdown you've been waiting for.

The Australian legend dominated Paris and now faces Oklahoma City's artificial rapids hunting her third Olympic title in this discipline. When Fox is dialed in, she makes navigating 20+ gates look like a Sunday paddle, but one touch means heartbreak.

contenders.name: Jessica Fox
contenders.note: Defending champion and overwhelming gold favorite

contenders.name: Ricarda Funk
contenders.note: Only paddler who consistently challenges Fox

contenders.name: Elena Lilik
contenders.note: Rising German star with lightning-fast gate technique

name: CSL03 Canoe Slalom
desc: Men's Kayak Single Semifinal and Final
blurb: The men's kayak throne is wide open after years of European dominance, making this final absolutely unmissable. Unlike the women's field where Fox reigns supreme, any of six paddlers could grab gold on Oklahoma City's unforgiving course. Expect gate touches, penalty seconds, and the kind of razor-thin margins that make canoe slalom finals legendary.

contenders.note: Tokyo champion seeking back-to-back golds

contenders.name: Giovanni De Gennaro

name: CSL04 Canoe Slalom
desc: M/W Canoe Single Heats - 1st and 2nd Run
blurb: Canoe singles — the sport's most brutal discipline where paddlers use just one blade and raw upper-body strength to muscle through gates. This is your chance to see the canoe slalom purists before the field narrows, including watching how athletes adapt their lines to Oklahoma City's unique flow patterns. The margin for error is even smaller than kayak, making every gate a potential drama.

contenders.name: Jessica Fox
contenders.note: Going for rare kayak-canoe double at same Games

contenders.name: Benjamin Savsek
contenders.note: Tokyo gold medalist and technical master

contenders.name: Elena Lilik
contenders.note: Young German equally dangerous in both boats

name: CSL05 Canoe Slalom
blurb: Jessica Fox hunting the kayak-canoe double that only she seems capable of achieving in modern slalom. The canoe single demands different muscles, different technique, and frankly, different levels of pain tolerance than kayak — yet Fox has mastered both like no athlete before her. This could be the performance that cements her as the greatest whitewater athlete in Olympic history.

contenders.name: Jessica Fox

contenders.note: Fox's biggest challenger in canoe discipline

name: CSL06 Canoe Slalom
blurb: Benjamin Savsek owns this discipline like Fox owns women's kayak — the Slovenian's gate precision in canoe single is absolutely surgical. But Oklahoma City's course could shake up the established order, and younger paddlers are hungry to dethrone the Tokyo champion. When the margins are measured in hundredths and gate touches cost two seconds, anything can happen.

blurb: Your first taste of kayak cross chaos — four boats, one finish line, and inevitable carnage. This new format turned Paris upside down and now Oklahoma City gets the treatment, with preliminary rounds that can eliminate medal favorites before they hit their stride. Joseph Clarke showed in Paris how head-to-head racing changes everything; expect crashes, blocks, and pure whitewater mayhem.

blurb: More kayak cross qualifying madness as the field gets cut down to medal contention. The beauty of these prelims is watching traditional slalom stars adapt to door-to-door racing — some thrive on the contact, others crumble under the pressure of having opponents inches away. Four-boat heats mean every paddle stroke matters, and one bad gate costs you everything.

When traditional slalom meets full-contact racing, legends are born and hearts are broken in real time.

This is it — the first Olympic cricket gold medal since 1900, and possibly the most watched cricket match in history. If India makes this final, a billion people will stop what they're doing to watch Virat Kohli chase Olympic immortality.

If Australia's here, they'll be hunting their sport's ultimate prize. Either way, you're witnessing the single most important cricket match of the modern era.

name: CRD01 Cycling Road (Time Trial)
blurb: The race of truth strips cycling down to its essence — no teammates, no tactics, just pure power against the clock on LA's streets. Watch Remco Evenepoel defend his Paris gold in the men's race, while Chloe Dygert gets her shot at redemption on home soil after years of injury battles. This is where legends are made in the loneliest discipline in cycling, with both titles decided in a single afternoon of suffering.

blurb: Free roadside viewing for one of cycling's most unpredictable events — the women's road race from Venice Beach through LA's hills. Kristen Faulkner shocked the world with her Paris gold, and now she gets to defend on home soil with the Pacific Ocean as her backdrop. This isn't just about the favorites; road races are tactical chess matches where a perfectly-timed breakaway or sprint can turn an unknown into an Olympic champion.

Tadej Pogačar arrives as the most dominant rider on the planet after his 2024 triple crown, but road races don't care about rankings. Mathieu van der Poel proved that at Worlds, and Remco Evenepoel showed it in Paris — on any given Sunday, tactics trump pure power.

The women's springboard final is where diving meets theater — you're close enough to hear every splash, see every grimace, feel every perfect entry. China's dominance isn't guaranteed here like on platform; springboard rewards rhythm and finesse over raw power.

The Rose Bowl Aquatics Center's outdoor setting adds the San Gabriel Mountains as a backdrop to six dives that will determine an Olympic champion. This is the event where underdogs can steal gold with a perfect list.

This outdoor venue transforms springboard diving; the wind, the sun, the mountain backdrop all become factors that separate pretenders from contenders.

Now they do it under the California sun with American crowds hoping for an upset. Six dives, one Olympic champion, and the outdoor drama of the Rose Bowl Aquatics Center amplifying every entry.

The women's 10m platform final — China has won every Olympic gold in this event since 2008, but Quan Hongchan versus Chen Yuxi might be the greatest platform duel in Olympic history.

China's dominance here feels even more inevitable than individual events, but synchronization adds variables that can't be controlled through training alone. When perfection meets human error at 30 mph, magic happens.

China's pairs have owned this event, but the field is hungry to break their stranglehold in front of what promises to be a raucous outdoor crowd.

name: EQU01 Equestrian
desc: Eventing Dressage Team & Indiv. - Day 1, Session 1
blurb: This is your gateway to understanding eventing — the sport's most complete test starts with dressage, where horses perform precision movements that look deceptively simple. This is THE moment lacrosse fans have waited over a century to see.

Michelle Gulyas will be hunting her second straight Olympic final, but the new format levels the playing field in ways we've never seen before. With only the top athletes advancing to Monday's medal showdown, expect tactical fencing and brutal laser-run splits as careers hang in the balance.

Fiji's march toward Olympic immortality hits its biggest obstacle here — no team has ever won three sevens golds, but these are the matches where legends are made. France's title defense and New Zealand's pride both hang in the balance over 28 minutes of pure intensity.

name: RU712 Rugby Sevens

blurb: Men's rugby sevens crowns its Olympic champion in Carson Stadium's primetime finale — this is where sporting history gets written. Fiji chases unprecedented three-peat immortality, while France defends their Paris triumph and New Zealand hunts redemption after Tokyo disappointment. Fourteen minutes for Olympic gold in rugby's most electric format — it doesn't get bigger than this.

You'll see two completely different approaches to harnessing wind power, from the explosive kite foiling to windsurfing's tactical chess match.

This is genuinely one of the most visually spectacular moments of the entire Olympics.

Amber Rutter will be hunting redemption after that controversial Paris call cost her gold, and you'll witness the precision that separates weekend shooters from Olympic legends. This is where careers begin and end — one wobble in your stance, one blink at the wrong moment, and four years of preparation disappear.

The morning qualifiers will whittle the field to six, then it's sudden-death elimination where a single missed clay sends you home empty-handed. Miss this and you've missed the most technically demanding shooting discipline at the Games.

Trap shooting is pure psychology — 75 clay pigeons launched at random angles while you stand 16 yards away with nowhere to hide. Nathan Hales dominated Paris with an Olympic record, but qualifying day is where legends get humbled by a single moment of doubt.

The outdoor Whittier Narrows range adds wind variables that indoor shooters never face, making this the ultimate test of adaptability. You're watching millimeter precision under Olympic pressure.

One missed disc and you're watching from the sidelines — this is the most unforgiving 25-shot sequence in Olympic sport. The outdoor Whittier Narrows setting adds wind gusts that make Paris look like practice.

contenders.note: Turkish heavyweight machine hunting his first Olympic gold at 130kg.

name: WRE02 Wrestling
blurb: The semifinals where legends are made and dreams die on the mat. Susaki should cruise through at 50kg, but watch the Greco-Roman battles — the 130kg and 60kg weight classes are wide open with multiple medal contenders still alive. The LA Convention Center's gladiatorial atmosphere reaches fever pitch as only four wrestlers per weight class remain.

contenders.name: Yui Susaki
contenders.note: Barring injury, she's in the final — question is by how much.

contenders.name: Kenichiro Fumita
contenders.note: Japan's Greco-Roman ace looking to reach his second Olympic final.

contenders.name: Riza Kayaalp
contenders.note: The veteran heavyweight's last realistic shot at Olympic gold.

name: WRE03 Wrestling
desc: M Greco-Roman 77kg & 97kg, W Freestyle 53kg: R16 and QF; Repechages for Day 1 events
blurb: The meat grinder continues with two of wrestling's most competitive weight classes. Men's 77kg and 97kg Greco-Roman are absolutely loaded with talent, while women's 53kg freestyle showcases the sport's incredible global depth. Plus repechage drama — wrestlers getting their last-chance lifeline to medal contention after earlier defeats.

contenders.name: Frank Stäbler
contenders.note: Three-time world champ making his final Olympic run at 77kg.

contenders.name: Akzhol Makhmudov
contenders.note: Kyrgyzstan's wrestling pride and 97kg world champion.

contenders.name: Akari Fujinami
contenders.note: Rising Japanese star looking to add Olympic gold to her world title.

name: WRE04 Wrestling
desc: M Greco-Roman 77kg & 97kg, W Freestyle 53kg Semis; Medal Matches: M GR 60kg, 130kg & W FS 50kg
blurb: Medal Monday arrives with six gold medals on the line — this IS the Olympic wrestling experience. Susaki claims her inevitable gold at 50kg, but the Greco-Roman finals at 60kg and 130kg could go any direction. The evening session format creates pure electricity as each final builds toward the ultimate moment.

contenders.name: Yui Susaki
contenders.note: Already warming up her gold medal bite.

contenders.name: Kenichiro Fumita
contenders.note: Japan's best shot at Greco-Roman gold on the biggest stage.

contenders.name: Riza Kayaalp
contenders.note: The aging giant's final Olympic moment at 130kg.

name: WRE05 Wrestling
desc: M Greco-Roman 67kg & 87kg, W Freestyle 57kg: R16 and QF; Repechages for Day 2 events
blurb: Wrestling's middle weights take center stage, and these are the divisions where technique meets power in perfect balance. The 67kg and 87kg Greco-Roman classes historically produce the sport's most tactical battles, while women's 57kg has become ridiculously competitive worldwide.

contenders.name: Mohammadhadi Saravi
contenders.note: Iran's Greco-Roman technician and reigning world champion at 87kg.

contenders.name: Luis Orta
contenders.note: Cuba's wrestling machine carrying the legacy of Mijain Lopez.

contenders.name: Tsugumi Sakurai
contenders.note: Japan's 57kg world champion hunting her first Olympic gold.

name: WRE06 Wrestling
desc: M Greco-Roman 67kg & 87kg, W Freestyle 57kg Semis; Medal Matches: M GR 77kg, 97kg & W FS 53kg
blurb: Six more golds decided as wrestling's middle-weight marathon concludes. Iran and Cuba should battle for Greco-Roman supremacy, while Japan's Sakurai looks to continue their wrestling dominance at 57kg. The Convention Center's intimate setting makes every throw, every pin attempt feel like it's happening inches from your seat.

contenders.name: Mohammadhadi Saravi
contenders.note: Iran's best bet for wrestling gold in LA.

contenders.name: Tsugumi Sakurai
contenders.note: Japan's 57kg world champion ready to graduate to Olympic gold.

contenders.name: Luis Orta
contenders.note: Carrying Cuba's heavyweight wrestling tradition into a new generation.

name: WRE07 Wrestling
desc: M Freestyle 57kg & 86kg, W Freestyle 62kg: R16 and QF; Repechages for Day 3 events
blurb: Freestyle wrestling finally arrives, and the sport completely changes energy. Gone are Greco-Roman's upper-body restrictions — now it's all-out combat with leg attacks and explosive takedowns. The 57kg and 86kg men's divisions are absolutely stacked, while women's 62kg features multiple world medalists still hunting Olympic glory.

contenders.name: Spencer Lee
contenders.note: Iowa legend making his Olympic debut on home soil at 57kg.

contenders.name: Hassan Yazdani
contenders.note: Iran's freestyle superstar and two-time Olympic medalist at 86kg.

contenders.name: Nonoka Ozaki
contenders.note: Japan's 62kg world champion in the prime of her career.

name: WRE08 Wrestling
desc: M FS 57kg & 86kg, W FS 62kg Semis; Medal Matches: M GR 67kg, 87kg & W FS 57kg
blurb: The freestyle semifinals deliver pure chaos — leg attacks flying, scrambles everywhere, and the sport at its most entertaining. Spencer Lee gets his home crowd moment at 57kg, while Hassan Yazdani hunts his second Olympic gold at 86kg. Plus three more Greco-Roman finals to cap the evening, including Japan's shot at multiple golds.

contenders.name: Spencer Lee
contenders.note: The Iowa superstar wrestling in front of his home country.

contenders.name: Hassan Yazdani
contenders.note: Iran's freestyle legend looking for gold number two.

contenders.name: Nonoka Ozaki
contenders.note: Japan's 62kg powerhouse ready to strike gold.

name: WRE09 Wrestling
desc: M FS 74kg & 125kg, W FS 68kg: R16 and QF; Repechages for Day 4 events
blurb: Here it comes — men's freestyle 74kg, the most competitive weight class in all of wrestling. This division has been absolute murder for decades, with world champions getting upset in early rounds regularly. Add 125kg's heavyweight freestyle drama and women's 68kg depth, and you've got wrestling's most unpredictable day.

contenders.name: Kyle Dake
contenders.note: Four-time world champ finally getting his Olympic shot at 74kg.

contenders.name: Mason Parris
contenders.note: American heavyweight with explosive power and surprising speed.

contenders.name: Amit Elor
contenders.note: Teen phenom turned world champion hunting Olympic gold at 68kg.

name: WRE10 Wrestling
desc: M FS 74kg & 125kg, W FS 68kg Semis; Medal Matches: M FS 57kg, 86kg & W FS 62kg
blurb: The 74kg semifinals will be absolute war — this weight class has no easy matches, no guaranteed outcomes. Kyle Dake carries American hopes on home soil, but the global depth here is insane. Meanwhile, Amit Elor should cruise to the 68kg final, and the heavyweight division delivers the raw power spectacle freestyle wrestling promises.

contenders.name: Kyle Dake
contenders.note: The veteran American's best and likely final shot at Olympic gold.

contenders.name: Amit Elor
contenders.note: American wrestling prodigy who's dominated at every level.

contenders.name: Mason Parris
contenders.note: Team USA's heavyweight hope with surprising athleticism.

name: WRE11 Wrestling
desc: M FS 65kg & 97kg, W FS 76kg: R16 and QF; Repechages for Day 5 events
blurb: The final day of wrestling quarterfinals brings 65kg and 97kg freestyle — two divisions where technique meets power in devastating combinations. Sadulaev should dominate 97kg if he's healthy, while women's 76kg has quietly become one of the sport's most competitive weight classes. Last chance to see wrestling's future stars before they hit the big stage.

contenders.name: Abdulrashid Sadulaev
contenders.note: The Russian Tank now competing for Uzbekistan — still unstoppable.

contenders.name: Yianni Diakomihalis
contenders.note: Cornell legend making his Olympic debut at 65kg.

contenders.name: Kennedy Blades
contenders.note: American powerhouse dominating the 76kg division.

name: WRE12 Wrestling
desc: M FS 65kg & 97kg, W FS 76kg Semis; Medal Matches: M FS 74kg, 125kg & W FS 68kg
blurb: Sadulaev should absolutely demolish the 97kg field — he's wrestling on a different planet than everyone else. The 65kg semifinals will be technical masterpieces, while women's 76kg delivers surprising depth and athleticism. Plus three more finals including the legendary 74kg division that produces the sport's most memorable moments.

contenders.name: Abdulrashid Sadulaev
contenders.note: Two Olympic golds, now hunting a third for Uzbekistan.

contenders.name: Kyle Dake
contenders.note: The 74kg legend potentially winning gold on American soil.

contenders.name: Kennedy Blades
contenders.note: Team USA's 76kg world champion ready for Olympic glory.

name: WRE13 Wrestling
desc: Repechages and Medal Matches: M FS 65kg, 97kg & W FS 76kg
blurb: Wrestling's grand finale features the sport's three most anticipated finals. Sadulaev claims his inevitable gold at 97kg, but 65kg could produce fireworks and women's 76kg has multiple medal contenders still alive. The perfect ending to wrestling's return to LA — raw, emotional, and absolutely unforgettable.

contenders.name: Abdulrashid Sadulaev
contenders.note: The overwhelming favorite to cap wrestling with freestyle gold.

contenders.name: Kennedy Blades
contenders.note: American wrestling's final gold medal hope of the Games.

contenders.name: Yianni Diakomihalis
contenders.note: Cornell's finest potentially delivering a perfect Olympic debut.
